questions.question: A lake fishery has inverse demand for fish P = 100 - Q and private marginal harvesting cost MPC = 20. Each unit harvested imposes crowding and stock depletion cost MEC = Q on other fishers. What is the efficient harvest?
questions.explanation: Efficiency sets demand equal to social marginal cost: 100 - Q = 20 + Q, so Q = 40. The open-access/private condition would produce Q = 80. A gives the unregulated output. C uses half the external cost. D subtracts external cost incorrectly.

questions.question: A grazing commons supports cattle. The marginal private benefit of cattle is MPB = 90 - Q, private marginal cost is $30, and each cow imposes marginal external damage MEC = 2Q through overgrazing. What herd size is efficient, and what does open access tend to do?

choices.A: Q = 60; open access is efficient because no one owns the land.
choices.B: Q = 20; open access tends to overuse the commons.
choices.C: Q = 30; open access tends to underuse the commons.
choices.D: Q = 45; open access equals the average of private and social outputs.

questions.explanation: Efficiency requires 90 - Q = 30 + 2Q, so Q = 20. Open access ignores the external damage and sets 90 - Q = 30, so Q = 60, causing overuse. A gives the private output. C uses only part of the external cost and gets the direction wrong. D invents an averaging rule.

questions.question: For a common resource, demand is P = 120 - Q, MPC = 40, and MEC = aQ. What happens to efficient use when a rises from 1 to 3?

choices.A: It falls from 40 to 20 because overuse damage rises.
choices.B: It rises from 40 to 80 because the resource becomes more valuable.
choices.C: It remains 80 because users ignore MEC.
choices.D: It falls from 80 to 40 because private cost rises.

questions.explanation: Efficiency requires 120 - Q = 40 + aQ, so Q = 80/(1 + a). When a = 1, Q = 40; when a = 3, Q = 20. The mechanism is a steeper MSC curve from greater congestion or depletion damage. B reverses the effect. C gives the open-access private quantity. D incorrectly says private cost rises.

questions.question: A city beach is non-excludable but rival when crowded. If the city introduces an entry permit that limits daily users from 1,000 to the efficient level of 700, what is the main comparative-static effect?

choices.A: Use falls, and congestion external costs fall because access is restricted to the efficient quantity.
choices.B: Use rises, because permits make the beach non-rival.
choices.C: Use remains 1,000, because rivalry prevents regulation.
choices.D: Use falls to zero, because excludability always eliminates consumption.

questions.explanation: A permit system can reduce open-access overuse by making access excludable and limiting use. B confuses excludability with rivalry. C incorrectly says regulation is impossible. D exaggerates the effect of access control.

questions.question: In a graph of a common pasture, private marginal cost is below social marginal cost, and demand slopes downward. Which area represents the welfare loss from open access overuse?

choices.A: The triangle between social marginal cost and demand over the units used beyond the efficient quantity.
choices.B: The rectangle of total revenue at the open-access quantity.
choices.C: The area below demand and above private marginal cost up to the efficient quantity.
choices.D: The vertical gap between private and social marginal cost at zero use.

questions.explanation: Overuse creates DWL on units for which social cost exceeds marginal benefit. B is revenue, not welfare loss. C is private surplus from efficient units. D is not generally the welfare loss and may be zero.

questions.question: A common-resource diagram shows the open-access equilibrium to the right of the efficient quantity. What graph feature explains this?

choices.A: Users respond to private marginal cost, while the upward vertical gap to social marginal cost reflects congestion or depletion costs imposed on others.
choices.B: Users respond to social marginal cost, while regulators respond to private marginal cost.
choices.C: Demand lies below social marginal cost at every positive quantity, so no use is efficient.
choices.D: The good is non-rival, so marginal cost is zero.

questions.explanation: Common resources are overused because individual users ignore costs they impose on other users. B reverses private and social decision rules. C is not implied by overuse. D describes pure public goods, not common resources.

questions.question: Why does open access to a rival resource tend to create a tragedy of the commons?

choices.A: Each user captures the full private benefit of use but bears only part of the social cost from depletion or congestion.
choices.B: Each user captures no benefit from use, so nobody uses the resource.
choices.C: The resource is non-rival, so additional use has zero marginal cost.
choices.D: The government always sets the price above marginal cost.

questions.explanation: With open access, users do not internalize the full cost their use imposes on others, so the resource is overused. B contradicts observed overuse. C describes public goods, not common resources. D is not the mechanism.

questions.question: Several fishing boats harvest from the same unregulated coastal area. Fish caught by one boat are unavailable to others, and no boat can be excluded. Which model applies?

choices.A: Public good, because no one can be excluded.
choices.B: Common resource, because the fishery is non-excludable but rival.
choices.C: Club good, because boats pay a membership fee.
choices.D: Positive externality, because fishing creates benefits for consumers.

questions.explanation: The fishery is a common resource: non-excludable but rival. A ignores rivalry. C adds a fee not in the scenario. D focuses on consumer surplus rather than the access problem.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes a public good from a common resource?

choices.A: A public good is non-excludable and non-rival; a common resource is non-excludable and rival.
choices.B: A public good is rival and excludable; a common resource is non-rival and non-excludable.
choices.C: A public good is always provided by government; a common resource is always privately owned.
choices.D: A public good is overused; a common resource is underprovided because of free riding.

questions.explanation: The key distinction is rivalry. Public goods are non-rival; common resources are rival. B reverses classifications. C defines goods by provider rather than characteristics. D reverses the usual market failures.

questions.question: For a lake, demand is P = 100 - Q, MPC = 20, and MEC = Q. Open access leads to Q = 80, while efficient use is Q = 40. What is the deadweight loss from overuse?
questions.explanation: MSC = 20 + Q. From Q = 40 to Q = 80, the gap between MSC and demand rises from 0 to 80. DWL = 0.5 × 40 × 80 = 1600. A uses half the correct height. C incorrectly uses an average gap of 60. D doubles the triangle.
